one_line_takeaway: A patch-differential taxonomy of OpenClaw's 470 security advisories shows that per-layer, per-call-site trust checks -- not memory-safety bugs -- are the dominant root cause, letting independently moderate-severity flaws in the Gateway/Node-Host chain, a lexical exec-allowlist, and unsigned skill distribution each escalate to full host compromise.
key_finding: Two structural conditions recur across the entire corpus and are the paper's central takeaway, stated explicitly in the Conclusion: (1) a pervasive closed-world assumption in each subsystem -- the exec allowlist assumed command representations were lexically enumerable, channel allowlists assumed sender-identity fields were immutable, and the context-assembly pipeline assumed strings entering the context window were information rather than potential instructions -- assumptions the authors state are invalidated by OpenClaw's open-world deployment model; and (2) the absence of unified inter-layer policy enforcement, meaning three independently moderate-severity Gateway/Node-Host advisories composed into a complete unauthenticated RCE chain because no single enforcement point observed the full call path from LLM tool invocation to Node-Host shell execution. Per the full 470-advisory corpus (Table 1, Apr 2026 snapshot), the Gateway WebSocket Interface is the single largest and highest-risk surface (121 advisories, 25.7% of total, 7 Critical + 47 High severity), closely followed by the Channel Input Interface (119 advisories, 25.3%), then Exec Policy Engine (77, 16.4%), Tool Dispatch Interface (69, 14.7%), Container Boundary (39, 8.3%, including 2 Critical + 14 High), Plugin & Skill Distribution (28, 6.0%), Agent Context Window (11, 2.3%), and three newly emergent surfaces with very low counts -- Host OS Interface (3), LLM Provider Interface (2), Inter-Agent Communication (1) -- which the authors flag as reflecting surface novelty rather than absence of risk.
core_contribution: An empirical, patch-differential security taxonomy of OpenClaw, an open-source AI agent runtime, built from the full corpus of 470 security advisories filed between Jan 31 and Apr 15, 2026. The paper organizes vulnerabilities along two independent axes: a 'system axis' (10 architectural attack surfaces: Channel Input Interface, Plugin & Skill Distribution, Agent Context Window, Gateway WebSocket Interface, Tool Dispatch Interface, Exec Policy Engine, Container Boundary, Host OS Interface, LLM Provider Interface, Inter-Agent Communication) and an 'attack axis' mapped to a six-stage 'OpenClaw Kill Chain' that adapts five MITRE ATT&CK-style tactics (Initial Access, Execution, Credential Access, Privilege Escalation, Impact) and introduces one novel stage, Context Manipulation, which the authors state has no analog in traditional intrusion frameworks because it exploits the LLM reasoning layer directly (controlling what the model believes, without executing code or bypassing a policy control). From this taxonomy the authors derive and evidence three principal findings, each traced to specific GHSA advisories and commit diffs: (1) a three-stage unauthenticated RCE chain spanning the Gateway and Node-Host subsystems (SSRF via an unvalidated gatewayUrl parameter -> bearer-token exfiltration via the same class of flaw in the agent tool layer -> node.invoke exposure of system.execApprovals.set allowing rewrite of the exec-approval policy, commits c5406e1, 2d5647a, 01b3226 / GHSA-g8p2, GHSA-gv46); (2) three independent, non-overlapping bypasses of the exec allowlist's lexical command-matching model -- line-continuation injection (commit 3f0b9db), busybox/toybox multiplexer dispatch (new module exec-wrapper-resolution.ts), and GNU long-option prefix abbreviation (commit 3b8e330) -- all invalidating the allowlist's closed-world assumption that command identity is recoverable by static text parsing; and (3) a malicious 'yahoofinance' skill distributed via the clawhub.ai registry (GitHub issue openclaw/openclaw#5675) that delivered a platform-differentiated two-stage dropper (Windows: AES-encrypted zip with Shannon entropy 7.944-7.960 bits/byte; macOS: base64-decoded curl-pipe-to-bash from a raw IPv4 address) entirely through LLM-context social engineering, bypassing the exec policy pipeline altogether. The paper also proposes a Defense Discussion (Section 6) mapping mitigations to each taxonomy branch, culminating in a cross-cutting recommendation for provenance-tagged, inter-layer policy enforcement rather than per-call-site trust checks.
threat_model: Adversary can introduce content into any of the wide set of input boundaries an OpenClaw deployment exposes: inbound messages on 15 chat/messaging platform adapters, third-party skills installed from the clawhub.ai registry or local filesystem, webhook payloads, and any data path that terminates in the LLM's context window (channel metadata, log headers, resource link fields, filesystem paths, inter-agent session routing). The adversary does not need memory-safety bugs or credential theft as a precondition -- the paper's central claim is that trust decisions are made per-layer/per-call-site with no unified enforcement point, so the adversary can achieve full host code execution (or LLM-context hijacking without any code execution at all, via the novel 'Context Manipulation' kill-chain stage) by chaining independently 'moderate-severity' flaws across layers, exploiting mutable platform identity fields as allowlist keys, or by having the LLM itself relay attacker instructions to the user with the same authority as operator-authored prompts.
limitations: Single-framework case study: the taxonomy is instantiated entirely on OpenClaw; the paper does not evaluate whether the two-axis taxonomy or the six-stage kill chain generalizes to other AI agent frameworks (no comparative corpus from a second framework is analyzed). The corpus arose from an unusually chaotic disclosure window -- the authors themselves note OpenClaw 'lacked a mature disclosure process' during rapid viral growth (200,000+ GitHub stars within weeks of a January 2026 relaunch), and three successive waves overlapped with active patching, which may bias severity/volume distributions relative to a framework with a mature, steady-state bug bounty process. Several taxonomy surfaces (Host OS Interface: 3 advisories, LLM Provider Interface: 2, Inter-Agent Communication: 1) rest on extremely small empirical samples; the authors themselves characterize these as 'emerging' and largely forward-looking rather than well-evidenced by the corpus. Section 5's deep-dive sub-analyses (e.g., 'Channel Input Interface: 35 advisories,' 'Gateway: 40 advisories,' 'Plugin & Skill: 7 advisories, 3.7%') are drawn from an earlier, smaller Feb 2026 snapshot (n=190) rather than the full n=470 corpus reported in Table 1/Section 3 -- the paper does not flag this inconsistency explicitly in the text, and a reader who conflates the Section 5 per-surface counts with the Section 3 full-corpus counts will double-count or misattribute figures. The proposed defenses in Section 6 (identity-anchored allowlists, URL provenance enforcement, semantic exec-allowlist interpretation, sandbox configuration validation, skill signing, context-provenance tagging) are discussed and motivated but not implemented or empirically evaluated by the authors; Section 6.6's provenance-model proposal draws on cited external work (StruQ, DataSentinel, PromptArmor, PromptSleuth) without independent validation against the OpenClaw corpus. No comparative or quantitative security evaluation (e.g., false-positive/negative rates of any proposed detection mechanism, or reproduction of exploits in a live testbed) is presented; the entire empirical basis is retrospective advisory/patch-diff analysis.

datasets_used: 470 GitHub Security Advisories (GHSA) filed against the open-source OpenClaw AI agent runtime, Jan 31-Apr 15 2026, across three disclosure waves (73 + 150 + 247 advisories)
GitHub issue openclaw/openclaw#5675 documenting the malicious 'yahoofinance' clawhub.ai skill

OpenClaw source-code patch diffs and commit history (e.g., commits c5406e1, 2d5647a, 01b3226, 3f0b9db, 3b8e330, 887b209, 85409e4, 6b4b604, e3b432e, 9e147f0) used as primary evidence, not a benchmark dataset
quantitative_results: Full corpus (Apr 2026, n=470): Gateway WebSocket Interface 121 advisories (25.7%), 7 Critical + 47 High severity -- largest surface by volume and by severity
Channel Input Interface 119 advisories (25.3%) across 15 messaging-platform adapters
Tool Dispatch Interface 69 advisories (14.7%), split into File & Process System (30), Sandbox Isolation (17), Browser Tooling (10) in the Section 5 sub-breakdown
Container Boundary 39 advisories (8.3%), including 2 Critical + 14 High
Host OS Interface 3 advisories (0.6%), LLM Provider Interface 2 (0.4%), Inter-Agent Communication 1 (0.2%) -- all newly emergent since the Feb 2026 snapshot

Feb 2026 interim snapshot (n=190, Table 1) used for the Section 5 close-reading case studies: Channel Input Interface 35 advisories (18.4%), Gateway WebSocket Interface 40 (21.1%), Exec Policy Engine 46 (24.2%), Tool Dispatch Interface 40 (21.1%), Container Boundary 17 (8.9%), Plugin & Skill Distribution 7 (3.7%), Agent Context Window 5 (2.6%) -- these are the earlier-snapshot figures underlying Section 5's per-surface advisory counts, not the full-corpus totals

Within the Channel Input Interface sub-pattern breakdown (Feb snapshot basis): allowlist authorization bypass via mutable identity fields = 13 advisories; webhook authentication failure = 10 advisories; channel-scoped disclosure/injection = 12 advisories
Malicious yahoofinance dropper: Windows payload archive blobs measured at Shannon entropy 7.944-7.960 bits/byte (consistent with AES-CBC/GCM encryption)
Docker sandbox-validation fix (commit 887b209): +691/-6 lines, adding a new 208-line validate-sandbox-security.ts module from scratch
